past_medical_history: Gastroparesis, diagnosed 2 years ago, managed with metoclopramide
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, controlled on oral agents
Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, well-controlled on lisinopril
Gastroesophageal reflux disease, diagnosed 5 years ago
Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees, worsening over past 3 years
Remote history of lower back surgery (L4-L5 laminectomy) 6 years ago

medications.frequency: as needed, up to four times daily

family_history: Father died at age 82 from congestive heart failure
Mother died at age 78 from complications of Alzheimer disease
Brother, age 73, has essential tremor managed with propranolol
No family history of Parkinson disease or other movement disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-years, quit 20 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional beer on weekends, approximately 2-3 drinks per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home; wife reports she has been doing more household tasks over the past year
social_history.exercise: Used to walk 2 miles daily but has reduced to short neighborhood walks due to unsteadiness
social_history.diet: Follows a diabetic diet with moderate adherence

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 25/30 — lost 2 points on delayed recall, 1 point on visuospatial/executive (clock drawing with mildly impaired spacing of numbers), 1 point on verbal fluency, and 1 point on abstraction. Speech is mildly hypophonic but content and fluency are preserved. No dysarthria.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial expression mildly hypomimic with reduced blink rate bilaterally. Mild bilateral reduction in facial expression symmetrically. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased bilaterally at wrists with subtle cogwheel rigidity on the right slightly more than left, best appreciated with reinforcement maneuver. No frank spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Mild bilateral bradykinesia on finger tapping, hand opening-closing, and toe tapping — slightly worse on the right but present bilaterally. Decrement in amplitude noted after 10 repetitions bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration mildly reduced at bilateral great toes (consistent with peripheral neuropathy, likely diabetic). Proprioception intact at toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly slow but accurate bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin slightly clumsy bilaterally but no clear cerebellar ataxia. Rapid alternating movements mildly slow bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait mildly narrow-based with reduced arm swing bilaterally, more so on the right. Stride length mildly decreased. No festination. Turns require 4-5 steps. Mild difficulty with tandem gait. Romberg negative. Pull test shows 2 steps of retropulsion but self-corrects. No freezing of gait observed.
neurological_exam.additional: No resting tremor observed during examination, including during distraction with mental tasks (serial 7s, months of year backward). Mild postural tremor noted in both hands with arms outstretched, slightly more prominent on the right, which the patient states has been present for years. Myerson sign (glabellar tap) positive — patient fails to habituate after 10 taps. No apraxia. Writing sample shows mildly reduced letter size but no frank micrographia.

patient.chief_complaint: Increasing difficulty with balance and slowness of movement over the past year
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sunita Reddy is a 64-year-old right-handed retired postal worker presenting to the neurology clinic with a one-year history of progressive gait unsteadiness and generalized slowness of movement. Her wife, who accompanies him, first noticed that she was walking more slowly approximately 12 to 14 months ago and seemed to be shuffling her feet at times, particularly in the morning. Over the past six months, she has become noticeably slower in her daily activities, taking longer to dress, button her shirts, and complete meals. Her wife reports that her handwriting has become slightly smaller, though she attributes this to arthritis in her hands. She has had two near-falls in the past three months, both occurring when turning quickly, though she has not fallen to the ground. She denies any frank tremor, but her wife has occasionally noticed a mild shaking of her hands when she holds the newspaper up to read. She endorses some difficulty with constipation that predates her current complaints by approximately two years, as well as reduced sense of smell that she became aware of about a year ago when she could not smell a gas leak that her wife detected. She has also noticed some difficulty initiating sleep and her wife reports that she has become more restless at night, occasionally kicking and flailing her arms during sleep, which began roughly 18 months ago. Her mood has been lower over the past year; she has less motivation to engage in hobbies such as woodworking and fishing that she previously enjoyed. She was started on metoclopramide approximately two years ago by her gastroenterologist for diabetic gastroparesis with nausea and early satiety, which has improved her gastrointestinal symptoms. She takes this medication regularly three times daily. She is referred today by her primary care physician who was concerned about possible Parkinson disease versus drug-induced parkinsonism given the metoclopramide use.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Scattered punctate and early confluent hyperintensities in periventricular white matter
signal_characteristics.T1: Isointense to mildly hypointense in corresponding regions

additional_observations: No basal ganglia infarcts or calcifications
Normal substantia nigra signal on susceptibility-weighted imaging
No abnormal enhancement following gadolinium administration
Mild mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses, incidental
mri.impression: Mild ventricular enlargement with Evans index at the upper limit of normal (0.31), with proportionate sulcal widening suggesting ex vacuo changes rather than hydrocephalus. No evidence of structural lesion or vascular parkinsonism. No findings to suggest multiple system atrophy or progressive supranuclear palsy. Age-appropriate mild cortical atrophy. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL (L), Sodium 153 mEq/L (H), Chloride 108 mEq/L (H), Glucose 133 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 7.3 % (H).

findings.region: Bilateral posterior putamina, more pronounced on the left
findings.signal: tracer_uptake: Asymmetrically reduced Ioflupane I-123 uptake in bilateral putamina with relative preservation of caudate heads. Left posterior putamen shows more pronounced reduction than right. Overall pattern is abnormal.

output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating bilateral reduction in dopamine transporter availability in the posterior putamina, more pronounced on the left, with relative preservation of the caudate nuclei. The asymmetry with left-sided predominance correlates with the slightly greater right-sided clinical findings. Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa Challenge Test.test: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (250 mg carbidopa/levodopa)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (250 mg carbidopa/levodopa) 18 points (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Post-levodopa UPDRS-III Motor Score (250 mg carbidopa/levodopa): 18 points (H)

findings.test: Head-up tilt test — Systolic BP drop
Head-up tilt test — Heart rate response

output.impression: Abnormal values: QSART — Distal leg 0.3 µL (L).

findings.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test — Delayed Recall

output.impression: Abnormal values: MoCA 25 /30 (L), Trail Making Test A (time) 41 seconds (H), Trail Making Test B (time) 116 seconds (H), Phonemic Verbal Fluency (FAS) 26 words in 3 minutes (L), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test — Delayed Recall 7 words (L), Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS-15) 7 /15 (H).

findings.value: Present — tonic and phasic EMG activity during 68% of REM epochs
Two episodes of arm flailing and vocalization during REM sleep observed by technician

quantitative_data.REM Without Atonia (RWSA): Present — tonic and phasic EMG activity during 68% of REM epochs
quantitative_data.Dream Enactment Behaviors: Two episodes of arm flailing and vocalization during REM sleep observed by technician

output.impression: Abnormal values: Total Sleep Time 289 minutes (L), Sleep Efficiency 72% % (H), REM Without Atonia (RWSA) Present — tonic and phasic EMG activity during 68% of REM epochs  (H), PLMI 17 events/hour (H), Dream Enactment Behaviors Two episodes of arm flailing and vocalization during REM sleep observed by technician  (H).

results.title: Drug-Induced Parkinsonism vs Idiopathic PD
results.summary: Metoclopramide is the most common cause of drug-induced parkinsonism (DIP) after antipsychotics. DIP typically presents with symmetric parkinsonism within weeks to months of drug initiation. Key distinguishing features from idiopathic PD include: symmetric presentation, absence of resting tremor, lack of prodromal symptoms (anosmia, RBD, constipation), and normal DaTscan.

results.title: Metoclopramide and Parkinsonism — FDA Black Box Warning
results.summary: FDA black box warning recommends against use of metoclopramide for more than 12 weeks due to risk of tardive dyskinesia. Risk of extrapyramidal symptoms including parkinsonism increases with duration and cumulative dose.

results.title: Unmasking of Subclinical PD by Dopamine-Blocking Agents
results.summary: Dopamine receptor-blocking agents such as metoclopramide can unmask subclinical Parkinson disease, making the clinical presentation appear earlier or more severe than it would otherwise. In these cases, DaTscan is abnormal, and symptoms persist or progress after drug discontinuation.

output.summary: Metoclopramide is a dopamine D2 receptor antagonist that can cause drug-induced parkinsonism (DIP) and can also unmask subclinical Parkinson disease. In this patient, the key question is whether parkinsonism is purely drug-induced or whether metoclopramide is unmasking underlying PD. Features favoring underlying PD include: prodromal symptoms (hyposmia, RBD, constipation), asymmetric findings on examination, positive DaTscan, and positive levodopa response. Metoclopramide should be discontinued regardless, and symptoms should be monitored for 6 months after discontinuation.

output.query: distinguishing Parkinson disease from drug-induced parkinsonism clinical features DaTscan

results.title: Differentiating Drug-Induced Parkinsonism from Parkinson Disease
results.key_finding: DIP is typically symmetric, develops within days to weeks of drug exposure, lacks non-motor prodromal features, and resolves within weeks to months after offending agent discontinuation

results.title: DaTscan Utility in Differentiating Neurodegenerative from Non-Neurodegenerative Parkinsonism
results.authors: Bajaj N, Hauser RA, Grachev ID
results.key_finding: DaTscan (Ioflupane I-123 SPECT) reliably distinguishes neurodegenerative parkinsonism (abnormal scan) from drug-induced parkinsonism (normal scan) with sensitivity 78-97% and specificity 80-100%

results.title: Prodromal Markers of Parkinson Disease
results.key_finding: REM sleep behavior disorder, hyposmia, constipation, and depression may precede motor symptoms by years. Presence of multiple prodromal markers strongly supports underlying synucleinopathy rather than drug-induced parkinsonism

output.summary: The literature supports a systematic approach to differentiating PD from DIP: (1) assess symmetry of findings — PD is typically asymmetric, DIP symmetric; (2) evaluate prodromal non-motor features — presence of RBD, hyposmia, and constipation favors PD; (3) DaTscan is the most useful ancillary test, with abnormal results confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit and effectively ruling out pure DIP; (4) discontinuation of the offending agent with clinical monitoring over 6 months is recommended regardless.

differential.likelihood: moderate — metoclopramide is a known cause; however, prodromal features and asymmetry argue against pure DIP
differential.key_features: 2-year metoclopramide use, bilateral presentation; BUT asymmetric exam, prodromal RBD/hyposmia/constipation argue against pure DIP

differential.likelihood: low — postural tremor present and family history of ET in brother, but bradykinesia and rigidity not seen in ET
differential.key_features: Bilateral postural tremor, brother with ET; however, bradykinesia, rigidity, and shuffling gait are not features of ET

differential.likelihood: low — gait difficulty present but MRI ventricular enlargement is proportionate to atrophy
differential.key_features: Gait instability, mild ventricular enlargement on MRI; however, no urinary incontinence, no magnetic gait, Evans index borderline, and sulcal widening proportionate

differential.likelihood: low — white matter changes present but no basal ganglia infarcts
differential.key_features: Hypertension, diabetes, periventricular white matter changes; however, no lower-body predominant parkinsonism, no stepwise progression, no basal ganglia infarcts

optimal_actions.action: Obtain complete history including medication review, focusing on dopamine-blocking agents and onset timeline relative to metoclopramide initiation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Metoclopramide started approximately 2 years ago; motor symptoms began approximately 1 year ago, but prodromal features (constipation, hyposmia, RBD) predate metoclopramide use

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with focus on asymmetry of motor findings, presence of resting versus postural tremor, and non-motor features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral bradykinesia and rigidity, slightly more on right; no resting tremor but mild postural tremor; hypomimia; positive Myerson sign

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild white matter changes and borderline ventricular enlargement but no structural lesion, no features of MSA or PSP

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, RPR to exclude metabolic/infectious causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal except mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c reflecting known diabetes

optimal_actions.action: Request DaTscan to differentiate neurodegenerative parkinsonism from drug-induced parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Abnormal — reduced bilateral putaminal dopamine transporter uptake, confirming presynaptic dopaminergic deficit

optimal_actions.action: Recommend discontinuation of metoclopramide and discuss alternative prokinetic agents with gastroenterologist
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Metoclopramide is exacerbating underlying PD; should be replaced with domperidone (if available) or non-dopamine-blocking alternative

optimal_actions.action: Consider levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive response (>30% improvement in UPDRS-III), supporting PD diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to confirm suspected REM sleep behavior disorder
Initiate dopaminergic therapy (low-dose carbidopa/levodopa or dopamine agonist) after metoclopramide discontinuation and washout
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement in bradykinesia, rigidity, and gait with levodopa therapy

optimal_actions.action: Screen for depression and refer for neuropsychological testing given borderline MoCA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild depression (GDS-15 = 7) and PD-MCI pattern on neuropsychological testing

optimal_actions.action: Refer to physical therapy for gait and balance training, and to occupational therapy for fine motor strategies

critical_actions: Identify metoclopramide as a dopamine-blocking agent that can cause or worsen parkinsonism
Recognize prodromal non-motor features (hyposmia, RBD, constipation) predating metoclopramide as evidence of underlying neurodegeneration
Order DaTscan to distinguish neurodegenerative parkinsonism from drug-induced parkinsonism
Assess for orthostatic hypotension and autonomic dysfunction to help rule out MSA
contraindicated_actions: Continuing metoclopramide without addressing its role in the parkinsonian presentation
Prescribing typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine) for any behavioral symptoms
Starting anticholinergic medications (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) as first-line treatment in a 70-year-old with cognitive concerns
Prescribing metoclopramide or prochlorperazine for nausea management
Diagnosing drug-induced parkinsonism without investigating for underlying neurodegenerative disease given the prodromal features
key_reasoning_points: The moderate difficulty lies in the confounding presence of metoclopramide: a clinician must determine whether the parkinsonism is drug-induced, neurodegenerative, or both (metoclopramide unmasking subclinical PD)
Prodromal non-motor features (hyposmia 1 year, RBD 18 months, constipation 2+ years, depression) strongly suggest an underlying synucleinopathy rather than pure drug-induced parkinsonism
The bilateral but asymmetric motor findings (right > left) support PD rather than DIP, which is typically symmetric
Absence of resting tremor does not exclude PD — approximately 25% of PD patients do not have tremor at onset (akinetic-rigid subtype)
DaTscan is the key discriminating test: abnormal in neurodegenerative parkinsonism, normal in drug-induced parkinsonism

The brother with essential tremor is a potential red herring — the postural tremor could initially suggest ET, but the presence of bradykinesia, rigidity, and non-motor features clearly points to a parkinsonian syndrome

Borderline ventricular enlargement on MRI could raise concern for NPH, but the proportionate sulcal widening and absence of classic NPH triad (gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, dementia) argue against it
The mild cognitive profile (PD-MCI with frontal-subcortical pattern) is consistent with early PD and is distinct from the cortical pattern seen in Alzheimer disease

metadata.educational_value: Distinguishing drug-induced parkinsonism from idiopathic Parkinson disease unmasked by a dopamine-blocking agent, with emphasis on prodromal non-motor features and role of DaTscan
